SEEA EA Physical Accounts

Lithuanian BBT5 — Curonian Lagoon and Baltic Sea Coast

MARBEFES WP4 | Generated: 2026-03-18 21:03

1. Introduction

This report presents the Physical Natural Capital Accounts for the Lithuanian Broad Biotope Type 5 (BBT5), covering the Curonian Lagoon and Baltic Sea coast. The accounts follow the UN System of Environmental-Economic Accounting — Ecosystem Accounting (SEEA EA) framework as adapted for marine environments under the MARBEFES project.

Three account components are presented:

  • Extent Account — area (hectares) per benthic habitat type
  • Condition Account — ecological condition per habitat type using EVA scores
  • Supply Table — ecosystem service provision proxies where data permits
Ecosystem Accounting Area (EAA): Lithuanian Exclusive Economic Zone, Territorial Sea, and Curonian Lagoon
Accounting period: 2017–2023 (monitoring data period)
Spatial resolution: 310 habitat polygons (HELCOM HUB Level 3 / EUNIS Level 2)

2. Input Data and Methodology

2.1 Habitat Classification

Benthic habitats were classified according to HELCOM HUB Level 3 / EUNIS Level 2, derived from the Lithuanian Environmental Protection Agency's 2019 habitat mapping. Six broad habitat types were identified in the Baltic Sea portion of the EAA.

2.2 Ecological Value Assessment (EVA)

Condition scores are derived from the MARBEFES EVA framework (Franco & Amorim, 2025), which assesses 15 Assessment Questions (AQ1–AQ15) across 5 ecosystem components: zoobenthos, fish, benthic habitats, zooplankton, and phytoplankton. Scores range from 0 (Very Low) to 5 (Very High). The EVA scores were computed using the MARBEFES EVA application v3.3 with sentinel-corrected data (September 2025).

2.3 Data Sources

ComponentSourceTypeCoverage
HabitatsLithuanian EPA habitat mapping (HELCOM HUB L3, 2019)Spatial polygonsFull EAA
BenthosSDM (Siaulys & Bucas, 2012) + video surveys (2021-2023)Modelled + observedBaltic Sea coast
FishICES BITS trawl surveys (2004-2023) + EPA catch statisticsSurvey CPUEBaltic Sea + Curonian Lagoon
ZooplanktonMRI surveys (1996-2020) + BIO-C3/RETRO projects (2014-2016)Station abundance21 stations
PhytoplanktonEPA monthly monitoring (2017-2023)Station biomass18 stations

2.4 Methodology

Extent: Computed from polygon areas (Shape_Area in m2, converted to Ha) grouped by MSFD broad habitat type.
Condition: EVA scores spatially joined to habitat polygons via centroid-in-hexagon matching. Mean score per indicator per habitat type.
Supply: Fish CPUE and plankton scores used as ecosystem service proxies (relative indices, not physical units).

3. Extent Account

The total marine habitat extent within the Lithuanian EAA is 219,624 hectares (2,196 km2). Sandy substrates dominate, covering nearly 43% of the seabed.

4. Condition Account

Ecological condition is assessed using EVA scores (0–5 scale) across five indicators. Each habitat polygon is assigned the EVA score of the hexagonal grid cell containing its centroid.

Note on confidence: All condition scores have Low confidence (each EC answered only 1–4 of 7–8 possible Assessment Questions). Scores should be interpreted as preliminary indicators, not definitive assessments.

5. Ecosystem Service Supply Table

The supply table presents available proxy indicators for ecosystem services per habitat type. Full SEEA EA compliance requires physical units (tonnes, m3, etc.); the values below are relative EVA scores (0–5) serving as provisional indicators.

5.1 Available Service Proxies

Habitat TypeFisheries (proxy score)Food Web Support (proxy)Primary Production (proxy)
Circalittoral coarse sedimentnan4.1352.128
Circalittoral mud0.8484.2931.981
Circalittoral rock and biogenic reef0.5374.1712.0
Circalittoral sand0.7944.2752.115
Infralittoral rock and biogenic reefnan3.8292.301
Infralittoral sandnan3.5072.931
Interpretation: Fish scores represent relative CPUE (Catch Per Unit Effort) across habitats, not absolute catch. NaN indicates no fish survey data for that habitat type (infralittoral and coarse sediment areas were not covered by bottom trawl surveys).

5.2 Data Gaps and Recommendations

The following ecosystem services require additional data for full physical accounting:

Ecosystem ServiceData StatusUnits NeededPotential Source
FisheriesProxy availabletonnes/yrLithuanian EPA fisheries statistics
Food Web SupportProxy availablemg C/m3HELCOM COMBINE monitoring
Primary ProductionProxy availableg C/m2/yrCopernicus Marine satellite remote sensing
Carbon SequestrationNOT AVAILABLEt C/ha/yrSediment core studies, IPCC factors
Coastal ProtectionNOT AVAILABLEwave attenuation indexSHYFEM hydrodynamic model
Tourism & RecreationNOT AVAILABLEvisits/yrTourism statistics, visitor surveys
Nutrient CyclingNOT AVAILABLEkg N-P/ha/yrHELCOM PLC, biogeochemical models

6. Summary and Conclusions

Key Findings

  • Extent: Sandy substrates (circalittoral + infralittoral sand) dominate at 43% of total area. Rock and biogenic reef habitats cover only 9.6% but are ecologically the most diverse.
  • Condition: Zooplankton scores are Very High across all habitats (3.5–4.3), dominating the overall condition assessment. Habitat diversity is highest in infralittoral rock/reef areas (Medium, 2.3) and lowest in circalittoral mud (Very Low, 0.6).
  • Supply: Fish provisioning data is available for only 3 of 6 habitat types (circalittoral only). Carbon sequestration, tourism, and nutrient cycling data are entirely absent.

Limitations

  • Condition scores have Low confidence due to limited AQ coverage per ecosystem component
  • No temporal baseline exists — this is a single-period snapshot, not a trend analysis
  • Curonian Lagoon habitats are not included (different classification system)
  • Supply table uses proxy scores, not physical units required by SEEA EA

Next Steps

  1. Acquire fisheries catch data in physical units (tonnes/year) per habitat type
  2. Integrate satellite-derived primary production estimates (Copernicus Marine)
  3. Establish temporal baselines for condition trend monitoring
  4. Extend to Curonian Lagoon habitats using the EUNIS Level 2 classification
  5. Connect with ARIES platform for automated natural capital accounting

References

  • Franco A. & Amorim E. (2025) Ecological Value Assessment (EVA) — Guidance including FAQs. MARBEFES WP4.1.
  • Razinkovas-Baziukas A. et al. (2025) Curonian Lagoon and Baltic Sea coast Lithuanian BBT EVA report. Klaipeda University / MARBEFES.
  • UN (2021) System of Environmental-Economic Accounting — Ecosystem Accounting (SEEA EA). United Nations.
  • Luisetti T. & Burdon D. (2023) Draft Guidance on Socio-Economic Frameworks and Methods — Physical Accounts Section. MARBEFES Deliverable D4.2.
